notes: Every entry in `expected` is a defect that was planted on purpose.
Every entry in `traps` is correct code that looks defect-adjacent. Reporting one is a false positive.
Elements are matched by their `data-gt` / `data-trap` attribute, not by selector text, so restyling the fixture does not break the eval.
`detectableBy` records which detection layer is supposed to find each issue. Recall is reported per layer, so `deterministic` mode is only ever scored against the issues it could possibly catch.

expected.summary: Alt text describes a football celebration; the image is a set dinner table.
expected.whyItMatters: The flagship case for the whole product. The alt attribute is present, fluent and confidently wrong, so every rule engine marks this element as passing. A screen-reader user is told something false rather than nothing, which is worse than a missing alt. Catching it requires comparing the pixels against the claim.

expected.summary: Informative image with no alt attribute at all.
expected.whyItMatters: The control case. Every rule engine catches this, so it proves the deterministic layer is wired up and that the baseline is not accidentally measuring nothing.

expected.summary: Email input labelled only by a placeholder — which rule engines pass.
expected.whyItMatters: The illustrative axe blind spot. Chromium computes the field's accessible name from the placeholder ('Email address'), so axe's label rule passes it and every rule engine reports it clean. But a placeholder is not a label: it vanishes the moment the field receives input, leaving a user who looked away with no clue what the field wanted. It takes judgment (ai.label-quality) to see that a present-but-fragile name is still a 3.3.2 failure — which is exactly the gap between a rule engine and this tool.

expected.summary: Body text at #949494 on white is about 3.0:1, under the 4.5:1 minimum.
expected.whyItMatters: Low contrast is the single most reported barrier in the wild, and it is fully mechanical, so there is no excuse for a tool to miss it.

expected.summary: A global `outline: none` removes the focus indicator from this button.
expected.whyItMatters: Static analysis of the stylesheet is not enough — the rule only matters if it actually wins the cascade for a focusable element. Handrail focuses the element, compares computed style, and falls back to a pixel diff with animations frozen.

expected.summary: Link text is "Click here", which carries no purpose out of context.
expected.whyItMatters: Screen-reader users routinely navigate by pulling up a list of every link on the page. In that list this one reads only "Click here", with the sentence that gave it meaning stripped away.

expected.summary: A fixed 720px-wide table forces horizontal scrolling at a 320px viewport.
expected.whyItMatters: Two-dimensional scrolling is punishing for anyone using magnification, which is a large share of low-vision users. This one is invisible at desktop width, which is why the viewport matrix exists.

expected.summary: Positive tabindex values put Contact before Menu, against the visual order.
expected.whyItMatters: Only walking the real tab sequence finds this. Reading the DOM in source order says the navigation is fine.

expected.summary: An 18x18 CSS-pixel button, under the 24x24 minimum.
expected.whyItMatters: A WCAG 2.2 addition, so most tooling has not caught up. The exception ladder matters here: inline targets and spacing-compensated targets are allowed, and a check that ignores that will drown users in false positives.

expected.summary: Required fields are indicated by red text colour alone.
expected.whyItMatters: Remove colour perception and the information is simply gone. There is no attribute to inspect — understanding that the colour *is* the message needs judgment.

expected.summary: The document has no lang attribute.
expected.whyItMatters: Without it a screen reader guesses the language, and an English page read with a French voice is close to unusable. Also the cheapest fix in the whole corpus, which makes it a good Tier-1 auto-fix target in Phase 4.

expected.summary: Opening hours are rendered as text inside an image.
expected.whyItMatters: The alt text says "Opening hours" and says nothing about the hours themselves, so the actual information is unreachable — and it cannot be resized, restyled or translated either.

expected.summary: The heading outline jumps from h1 to h3.
expected.whyItMatters: Heading navigation is how many screen-reader users skim a page. A hole in the outline reads as a missing section.

expected.summary: The newsletter dialog intercepts Tab and Shift+Tab and cycles focus between its own two controls, with no Escape handler and no reachable close control.
expected.whyItMatters: The worst failure mode there is: a keyboard user cannot leave, and the only way out is to reload the page and lose their place. It cannot be found by reading markup — it takes actually pressing Tab.

traps.summary: A genuinely decorative flourish, correctly hidden with alt="".
traps.whyItMatters: The commonest AI false positive in this space: an empty alt looks like a missing alt to a model that has not been told the difference.

traps.summary: #767676 on white is 4.54:1 — grey, but passing.
traps.whyItMatters: Sits just over the line. A check that eyeballs "this looks light" instead of computing the ratio fails here.
Boundary case. Off-by-one in the comparator shows up here and nowhere else.

traps.summary: A select with a correctly associated label element.
traps.whyItMatters: Proves the label check follows htmlFor/id rather than assuming any control near text is unlabelled.

traps.summary: Keeps a strong focus ring via :focus-visible despite the global outline reset.
traps.whyItMatters: The focus-visible check has to resolve the real cascade for this element, not just grep the stylesheet for `outline: none`.
